text:
HEAD AND NECK MEDICAL ONCOLOGY / RADIATION ONCOLOGY
SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE — 18 MONTHS POST-TREATMENT

ATTENDING: [Medical Oncology / Radiation Oncology Co-Signature]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine 18-month post-chemoradiation surveillance visit. Patient presents for follow-up of HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Chen is a 53-year-old Chinese-American female, never-smoker, originally diagnosed with T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition, p16+ classification) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue at age 52. She completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation — IMRT to 70 Gy in 35 fractions (SIB technique) to the primary and involved right level IIA node, with elective nodal volumes to 56–59.5 Gy — plus concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks x2 cycles (both cycles delivered at full dose). She completed her full course of treatment approximately 18 months ago without any radiation breaks.

Her treatment course was notable for grade 3 mucositis with severe odynophagia requiring PEG tube placement in week 5 of radiation, grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the bilateral neck, grade 1 xerostomia, and grade 2 fatigue. She was dependent on PEG tube feeds through the acute post-treatment period. A 12-week post-treatment FDG-PET/CT demonstrated complete metabolic response with no residual FDG-avid disease at the primary or nodal sites. PEG tube was removed approximately 5 months post-treatment once she re-established adequate oral intake. She has been followed in combined surveillance since then.

At the 12-month surveillance visit, CT neck with contrast showed only stable post-radiation changes and a small fibrotic right level IIA node (0.8 cm, significantly reduced from 2.4 cm at diagnosis) — no evidence of recurrence. Audiogram at that visit demonstrated mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in the 4–8 kHz range bilaterally, attributed to cisplatin. Thyroid function had normalized following an upward adjustment of her levothyroxine dose.

Today, she is presenting for her 18-month surveillance visit. She reports feeling well overall. She has returned to full-time work as a high school music teacher and has resumed her normal social activities. She denies any new neck masses, throat pain, hemoptysis, unexplained weight loss, or significant dysphagia. She has no new neurologic symptoms.

Regarding her residual late effects: xerostomia persists at a grade 1 level and remains her most bothersome ongoing complaint. She uses over-the-counter saliva substitutes (Biotene spray and gel) and sips water throughout the day. She was previously trialed on pilocarpine 5 mg TID starting at the 6-month visit, which she reports continued to provide partial subjective benefit. She is comfortable continuing this regimen. She denies significant trismus — mouth opening is subjectively preserved. Neck range of motion is maintained; she has been doing her home stretching exercises consistently.

Dysphagia has markedly improved. She eats a regular diet with only minor adaptive strategies — she avoids very dry or hard-to-chew foods and tends to moisten foods. She denies any frank aspiration events and reports that swallowing feels near-normal in her day-to-day life. Speech-language pathology cleared her to advance diet at the 12-month mark and she has not required further formal SLP sessions.

Hearing: She reports no subjective worsening of hearing since the last audiogram and has not required hearing aids. She is aware this will be monitored annually.

Skin and neck: The bilateral neck skin hyperpigmentation has continued to fade. No ulceration, lymphedema, or tightness of concern.

Dental: She is compliant with daily fluoride tray applications as instructed by dental oncology. No new dental complications reported. No jaw pain.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive for: chronic xerostomia (grade 1, stable), mild effort dysphagia with very dry foods (managed behaviorally), intermittent dry/scratchy throat sensation, mild high-frequency hearing impairment (known, stable).
Negative for: new neck mass or swelling, odynophagia, hemoptysis, unintentional weight loss, fever, chills, night sweats, shortness of breath, chest pain, new skin lesions on the neck, significant fatigue, trismus, vocal change or hoarseness, epistaxis, otalgia, or neurologic complaints. No mood or depressive symptoms endorsed today.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and pleasant.
Vital signs: BP 118/74, HR 68, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 138 lbs (within approximately 2 lbs of pre-treatment baseline).
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose: Atraumatic, normocephalic. Oropharynx inspected: Bilateral neck skin with resolving post-radiation hyperpigmentation, no moist desquamation, no active dermatitis. Oral mucosa moist (patient had hydrated prior to visit); mild atrophy of buccal mucosa consistent with xerostomia. Dentition intact with fluoride trays in routine use.
Flexible nasopharyngoscopy (performed by ENT colleague in co-evaluation): Right base of tongue — mucosa appears well-healed, with subtle post-radiation mucosal pallor/atrophy. No discrete mass, ulceration, exophytic lesion, or neovascularization identified at the primary site or at the right glossoepiglottic fold. Left base of tongue: unremarkable. Vallecula clear. Epiglottis normal appearance. Hypopharynx and larynx: no masses, symmetric vocal cord mobility.
Neck: Supple, full range of motion. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally — levels I through V assessed. No submandibular masses. No carotid bruits appreciated on auscultation (noted for future surveillance given radiation exposure to carotids). Right level IIA region: soft, no palpable residual node.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. PEG tube site healed without complication.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. No facial asymmetry, tongue deviation, or shoulder weakness.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS
Most recent labs reviewed (from prior visit / pre-visit draw):
- CBC: WBC 6.1 k/uL, Hgb 13.4 g/dL, Plt 228 k/uL — within normal limits.
- BMP: Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, BUN 14, electrolytes within normal limits, glucose 92 mg/dL.
- TSH: 2.4 mIU/L — within normal limits on adjusted levothyroxine (dose was increased at 6-month visit for radiation-induced hypothyroidism; thyroid function has remained well-controlled since).
- Magnesium: 1.9 mEq/L — low-normal; no supplementation currently required.

Audiogram (deferred to annual check at 24-month visit per plan; no subjective worsening, not repeated today).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT neck with contrast obtained prior to today's visit for 18-month surveillance:
Stable post-radiation changes in the oropharynx. No new mass, abnormal enhancement, or suspicious lesion at the right base of tongue. Bilateral cervical lymph nodes unremarkable — right level IIA node now 0.6 cm (stable to slightly decreased from 0.8 cm at 12-month scan; fibrotic, no pathologic features). No new lymphadenopathy at any nodal station. No unexpected chest or upper abdominal findings on included cuts. Radiologist impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Post-treatment changes stable.

PET/CT not performed at this interval (18 months); per radiation and medical oncology consensus, surveillance CT neck has been adequate given confirmed complete metabolic response at 12-week post-treatment PET/CT and no clinical concerns for recurrence. FDG-PET deferred unless clinically indicated.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC (right base of tongue, T2N1M0), 18 months post-definitive concurrent chemoradiation — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE.
   - Clinical exam and flexible nasopharyngoscopy by ENT: no residual or recurrent tumor at primary site or regional nodes.
   - CT neck: stable post-radiation changes, no new or enlarging lesions. Right level IIA node fibrotic and decreasing.
   - Patient counseled extensively: she is at an 18-month disease-free landmark for HPV-positive OPSCC, which carries an excellent prognosis. The majority of recurrences in this population, if they occur, happen within the first 2–3 years, and she has had none. Her 5-year overall survival probability in this subgroup (p16+, complete responder, T2N1M0, never-smoker) exceeds 85–90%.
   - Surveillance will continue per NCCN HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC guidelines: H&P with nasopharyngoscopy every 2–4 months; next interval CT neck at 24-month mark.
   - Patient instructed to call immediately if she notices a new neck mass, persistent worsening sore throat, hemoptysis, or other concerning symptoms.

2. Late-effect xerostomia (grade 1, chronic).
   - Expected consequence of bilateral parotid irradiation (mean parotid dose: right 28 Gy, left 24 Gy). Stable and manageable at current level.
   - Continuing pilocarpine 5 mg TID and Biotene spray/gel as needed.
   - Reinforce daily hydration. Dental hygiene with fluoride tray application to continue — critical for xerostomia-related caries prevention.
   - Will discuss acupuncture for xerostomia at next visit if symptoms remain bothersome (data supportive in post-radiation head and neck patients).

3. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism — stable, managed.
   - TSH 2.4 mIU/L on current levothyroxine dose. Dose adjustment not needed today.
   - Annual TSH monitoring to continue; will check at 24-month visit.

4. Cisplatin-associated hearing loss — mild, bilateral high-frequency SNHL.
   - Not affecting daily function or professional activities. No hearing aids required at this time.
   - Annual audiogram planned at 24-month follow-up.

5. Carotid artery surveillance.
   - Patient counseled on the well-established long-term risk of radiation-induced carotid stenosis (bilateral carotid territory received significant dose). She has no current symptoms of TIA or stroke.
   - Baseline carotid duplex ultrasound recommended at or around the 2-year mark per our institutional late-effects protocol.

6. Dental late effects.
   - Patient compliant with fluoride tray regimen. No osteoradionecrosis symptoms (jaw pain, exposed bone, non-healing socket). Tooth #19 extraction site fully healed per prior dentistry report.
   - Routine dental care every 6 months; dental oncology to co-manage. Patient reminded to inform all future dentists and oral surgeons of her radiation history and the need for antibiotic prophylaxis and careful planning prior to any future dental extractions.

7. General health and wellbeing.
   - Patient has returned to full-time teaching and social activities — excellent quality of life and functional recovery.
   - Weight within 2 lbs of pre-treatment baseline — full weight recovery achieved.
   - ECOG PS 0.
   - Encouraged to maintain regular exercise and healthy diet. Continued sun protection of the neck skin.

Return to clinic in approximately 3–4 months for next surveillance interval visit. Patient verbalized understanding of plan and expressed no concerns at today's visit.

[Electronically signed — Medical Oncology / Radiation Oncology]
[Co-attestation: Radiation Oncology, Dr. Jennifer Park]
